input.user: To whom it may concern. I have been a customer of Acme Co for over four years and over the last six months the service has noticeably degraded. input.user: Hi Acme Co, I'm looking at this month's invoice and I was charged twice for the same subscription — once at 02:14 and again at 02:16, same card, same amount. Looking at the timestamps it really looks like your billing job retried after a transient failure and didn't dedupe on the idempotency key (we've seen this exact pattern in other Stripe-backed systems). I don't actually mind waiting for the refund, but you almost certainly have other customers hit by the same bug today and may not know it yet. Account customer@example.com. — Customer B

tier_2_judge.criteria: Reply acknowledges the duplicate charge on the customer's account and signals the report will be looked into, without confirming a systemic bug exists or promising a refund or fix.

metadata.notes: Hard: reads partly like a technical bug report (idempotency key, retry behaviour, suggestion that other customers are affected), but the actionable issue for this customer is a duplicate charge on their invoice. Dominant theme is the billing impact on the customer's own account, so 'billing' is correct over 'technical'.

The customer's framing ('rough month', 'overall experience') makes the dominant theme dissatisfaction with the holistic experience rather than any individual item, so 'complaint' is the correct top-level classification. Note that production routing systems may legitimately route this to billing or technical first based on actionability — where models choose those classifications, treat as legitimately contestable rather than scoring as wrong.
